summarization.a: XRD patterns of Cu/PEALD TiN (10 nm)/Si after annealing from 450 °C to 600 °C. Cu(111), Cu(200), and Cu(220) peaks grow with temperature. This shows loss of barrier stability at high temperature.
summarization.b: XRD patterns of Cu/PEALD Ti–Si–N (10 nm)/Si from no annealing to 700 °C. Cu peaks appear later and grow more slowly than in TiN. This shows improved thermal stability of Ti–Si–N.

a.question: How does the evolution of Cu diffraction peaks with annealing temperature in subfigure (a) reflect the structural integrity of the TiN diffusion barrier?
a.answer: The increasing intensity of Cu(111), Cu(200), and Cu(220) reflections with annealing temperature indicates copper grain growth and diffusion through the TiN barrier. This suggests that the TiN layer loses its barrier functionality at elevated temperatures, allowing Cu atoms to penetrate and crystallize on the silicon substrate. The rapid peak emergence above 550 °C reflects limited thermal stability of the PEALD TiN barrier.

a.question: At what annealing temperature does Cu diffusion become clear in subfigure (a)?
At what annealing temperature range does copper diffusion become clearly evident through the TiN barrier in subfigure (a)?
a.answer: Copper diffusion becomes clearly evident between 550 °C and 600 °C.

b.question: How do the XRD results in subfigure (b) support the suitability of TiSiN as a diffusion barrier for Cu interconnect applications?
b.answer: The delayed appearance and reduced intensity of Cu diffraction peaks demonstrate that TiSiN effectively suppresses copper diffusion during high-temperature annealing. This indicates superior barrier performance compared to TiN, maintaining interfacial stability up to 700 °C. Such thermal robustness makes TiSiN more suitable for backend interconnect processes requiring high-temperature reliability.
